messages.content:
hi everyone!

Introducing SAIRCommunityBench_v1

Dataset is available here: https://gist.github.com/stokasz/81b80326a3e957caddef6c136ff3e4be

I've been working on curating a new dataset for the challenge. I was annoyed that no matter what dataset I tested my cheatsheet against, there was hardly any generalization. Especially cheatsheets that have saturated previous datasets on GPT-OSS-120b and GPT-OSS-20b always needed to be remade for the new ones. That's not really general algebraic knowledge this challenge is looking for.

After 4 days of working on my classification infra, I'm excited to present you the SAIRCommunityBench_v1. It was distilled from a full new 2000 magmas dataset, into the same format as all the other SAIR datasets. If somebody needs the full 2000 equations dataset, it's available here:

https://gist.github.com/stokasz/b98356e00186b92f5ac10c8dd7b9be22

SAIRCommunityBench_v1 has:
- Balanced TRUE/FALSE implication rows, with the standard submission slice at exactly 100/100
- A wider mix of law lengths: mostly 4-op and 5-op rows, plus 6-op tail
- Positives and negatives from multiple source families, including Lean-backed implications and explicit finite, SMT, polynomial, linear, infinite-style, and Austin-style counterexample regimes.

I've tested frontier SOTA models on the benchmark, on default reasoning. The most interesting results are of course GPT-OSS-120b & 20b, and Llama 70b with the (now hopefully generalized for a wider magmas distribution) algebraic cheatsheets. Best one, GPT-OSS-120b scores 78.5% on a new benchmark. 

If my results are correct, the implications are that smaller models are capable of generalized, strong mathematical reasoning, if given the correct representation (what SAIR is looking for). I'd assume that these results point towards an interesting, previously undiscovered capability of models - given the right representation, they can solve much more complex mathematical problems than a non-representation benchmarking done by top AI labs suggests.

I'd urge researchers to try benchmarking smaller models on frontier math problems and the world's hardest benchmarks, using the method with representations in small cheatsheets. Actually this is also a research direction I'd like to pursue next.

These are a truly fascinating results in my opinion! I didn't expect the cheatsheet generalization to hold, and 20b GPT OSS still scoring better than SOTA 1T+ models.

I'd ask for the community-driven re-verification of the results and probing the dataset, so we can create the v2 of it. The goal should be the creation of the hardest and most widely distributed magmas benchmark, to test the limits of cheatsheets.

Full benchmark results:

[SAIRCommunityBench_v1_BENCHMARK_CHART.png](/user_uploads/5/21/Y1nE8tmn6kQ1jOVlARUOItcx/SAIRCommunityBench_v1_BENCHMARK_CHART.png)

What's even more interesting is that the cheatsheet that scores best for GPT-OSS-120b also gets near benchmark saturation on the hard_200! :tada:
[image.png](/user_uploads/5/35/YDVkOXNWmtPAEkIg4aAgW--s/image.png)
Post on X for additional public exposure to the challenge and discussion: https://x.com/stokasz/status/2035715256618475712
@**stokarz** How can I interpret the difficulty composition of this dataset, if possible wrt. the existing datasets: normal, hard and your previously submitted one (ultrahard?)
It would be very useful to know how this data was produced/procured. What distribution of equations? What techniques were used to prove/disprove implications?
it was derived from the results of Equational Theories Project. i haven't proved anything "new" myself. Just curated data in a specific way, so that the distribution of equations could be the widest.

@_**Baptiste Polchlopek|97** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/13-Math-Distillation-Challenge---equational-theories/topic/SAIRCommunityBench/near/1077):
```quote
@**stokarz** How can I interpret the difficulty composition of this dataset, if possible wrt. the existing datasets: normal, hard and your previously submitted one (ultrahard?)
```

The goal of this dataset is to be the hardest published to date. As you can see, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and other models that scored 90%+ on hard_200 before, are getting close to 50% on this one.

@_**stokarz|50** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/13-Math-Distillation-Challenge---equational-theories/topic/SAIRCommunityBench/near/1079):
```quote
it was derived from the results of Equational Theories Project. i haven't proved anything "new" myself. Just curated data in a specific way, so that the distribution of equations could be the widest.
```

Okay, but derived /how/? ETP had almost entirely at-most-4-op equations, and just a small scattering of higher op equations, usually included in the database because they were important or interesting for some very special reason. Was this just taken from the 5- and 6-op equations that are there in the data? Or were you generating any new datapoints (by e.g. running Vampire or checking small magmas)?

Additionally, I would recommend that participants start incorporating more of a data science approach to this problem:

Given that prompt+cheatsheet is being optimized on a fixed dataset (`train` dataset split analogy), many iterations can increase the apparent performance without much generalization gains, as dataset specifics start being fed into the input prompt.

While benchmarking the same dataset on other models (through playground or not) can give some sense of generalization, I think this generalization is more of a "reusable manner to write hints" than a indicator of how well new problems can be handled.

Perhaps having a `validation` dataset containing implications that are unrelated (or less related) to the `train` dataset could give a better signal for generalization on new implications than "optimizing a prompt on *normal*, then checking on *hard* only to see poor performance".

Both `train` and `validation` datasets could be crafted such that they contain implications of various difficulties, and only constructed during evaluation. For example, splitting each dataset (normal, hard, ultrahard...) in 2, one half for `train`, another for `validation`. 

Furthermore, if all proven implications are grade-able, this could evolve into a stratified dataset splitter, where both `train` and `validation` are balanced in terms of (and randomized with a seed):
- difficulty (number of steps/operations needed)
- true vs. false
- *implicit vs. explicit proof*
...

@_**Alex Meiburg|134** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/13-Math-Distillation-Challenge---equational-theories/topic/SAIRCommunityBench/near/1081):
````quote
@_**stokarz|50** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/13-Math-Distillation-Challenge---equational-theories/topic/SAIRCommunityBench/near/1079):
```quote
it was derived from the results of Equational Theories Project. i haven't proved anything "new" myself. Just curated data in a specific way, so that the distribution of equations could be the widest.
```

Okay, but derived /how/? ETP had almost entirely at-most-4-op equations, and just a small scattering of higher op equations, usually included in the database because they were important or interesting for some very special reason. Was this just taken from the 5- and 6-op equations that are there in the data? Or were you generating any new datapoints (by e.g. running Vampire or checking small magmas)?
````

Hi Alex, the goal of this was to not specifically curate a dataset that's mathematically interesting for humans, but rather the one that would be extremely hard for the SOTA LLMs, and then if one can find the prompt - would the same prompt be used for saturating other benchmarks (present or whatever comes in the future). Bcs I was frustrated with the previous datasets being "so easily" saturated. 

I didn't do any new mathematics, my contribution is mostly extraction, deduplication, labeling and selection. yes, benchmark includes many datapoints from automated proof/countermodel pipelines. In practices, the benchmark is the combination of initial SAIR benchmarks (old deduplicated 69, hard_200, ultrahard FALSE ones) plus with extracted results from ETP branches such as full-spectrum and joachim/z6plus, sources like All4x4Tables, FinSearch, VampireProven, Z3, EquationSearch, and FinitePoly,plus local JSONL exports of generated ETP theorem/countermodel files: SmallMagmas, FiniteModel, Generated/Greedy, SimpleRewrites, TrivialBruteforce, and MagmaEgg

@_**Baptiste Polchlopek|97** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/13-Math-Distillation-Challenge---equational-theories/topic/SAIRCommunityBench/near/1083):
```quote
Additionally, I would recommend that participants start incorporating more of a data science approach to this problem:

Given that prompt+cheatsheet is being optimized on a fixed dataset (`train` dataset split analogy), many iterations can increase the apparent performance without much generalization gains, as dataset specifics start being fed into the input prompt.

While benchmarking the same dataset on other models (through playground or not) can give some sense of generalization, I think this generalization is more of a "reusable manner to write hints" than a indicator of how well new problems can be handled.

Perhaps having a `validation` dataset containing implications that are unrelated (or less related) to the `train` dataset could give a better signal for generalization on new implications than "optimizing a prompt on *normal*, then checking on *hard* only to see poor performance".

Both `train` and `validation` datasets could be crafted such that they contain implications of various difficulties, and only constructed during evaluation. For example, splitting each dataset (normal, hard, ultrahard...) in 2, one half for `train`, another for `validation`. 

Furthermore, if all proven implications are grade-able, this could evolve into a stratified dataset splitter, where both `train` and `validation` are balanced in terms of (and randomized with a seed):
- difficulty (number of steps/operations needed)
- true vs. false
- *implicit vs. explicit proof*
...
```

it's would the best scenario, but the problem is that whatever narrower / not related dataset i curated, you could saturate it with the cheatsheet, but then it wouldn't generalize to other parts of magmas distribution.
so idk how to solve this exact problem, but hope that this benchmark can help the others to generalize better

Another idea I had was iterative generalizing, but I'm not confident about it:
Optimizing the process on equational laws with at most **3** magma ops and checking on those up to **4**.
Then, for a result that happens to do well on **4**, reuse the process to craft a prompt for them (being very hopeful that this translates to good result when checking on **5** magma ops)
Essentially, if 3=>4 works well, can I expect the same from 4=>5

Hi @**stokarz** How did you verify whether the implications were true or false? Did you scrape purely from known implications? Did you verify some with an ATP? Do you have certificates (or at least citations to other proofs of correctness?).

@_**Damek Davis|31** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/13-Math-Distillation-Challenge---equational-theories/topic/SAIRCommunityBench/near/1095):
```quote
Hi @**stokarz** How did you verify whether the implications were true or false? Did you scrape purely from known implications? Did you verify some with an ATP? Do you have certificates (or at least citations to other proofs of correctness?).
```
Thanks for the question Damek. I have only used known implications.
Can you add citations to the known implications so we can verify? Are they from the ETP database? Are they external?
Sure, will add these

@_**Damek Davis|31** [said](https://zulip.sair.foundation/#narrow/channel/13-Math-Distillation-Challenge---equational-theories/topic/SAIRCommunityBench/near/1099):
```quote
so
```
95 rows are SAIR-Hugging-Face (derived from the previous datasets), and the remaining 105 are directly backed by public upstream ETP proof/countermodel.

here you go: https://gist.github.com/stokasz/db2c18514d0a59e2b213de23c8d0906c

> plus with extracted results from ETP branches such as full-spectrum and joachim/z6plus, sources like All4x4Tables, FinSearch, VampireProven, Z3, EquationSearch, and FinitePoly,plus local JSONL exports of generated ETP theorem/countermodel files: SmallMagmas, FiniteModel, Generated/Greedy, SimpleRewrites, TrivialBruteforce, and MagmaEgg

Okay, this is exactly what I was wondering. :D Thanks! I was just trying to get a picture of where this came from haha
Cool!
